text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic truncal vagotomy, hiatal hernia repair, and subtotal gastrectomy with Bilroth II reconstruction. The lesser omentum below the hiatus is divided using a ligature, revealing the right corner of the diaphragm. The peritoneum anterior to the esophagus is divided and extended laterally, exposing the superior aspect of the left crura. The right crura is further exposed, and the posterior vagus nerve is identified and transected. A segment of the posterior vagus nerve is sent for frozen section to pathology, confirming the presence of nerve fibers.

The surgeon identifies the anterior left vagus nerve and excises a segment using ligature. The excised segment is sent to pathology for frozen section, confirming the presence of anterior vagus nerve fibers. The surgical team searches for additional branches but finds none.

The esophagus, with an NG tube in place, is retracted to the left, freeing it posteriorly and exposing the left crura of the diaphragm. The mobilized esophagus is moved intra-abdominally, and the hiatus is tightened with a suture of 2-0 silk, narrowing the opening to prevent further herniation.

The surgeon performs a subtotal gastrectomy by dividing the gastrocolic omentum near the greater curvature of the stomach, carefully preserving the gastroepiploic arcade. The dissection progresses from the lower antrum upwards to the hemigastrectomy point, continuing along the greater curvature until reaching the first portion of the duodenum, just before the gastroduodenal artery.

The surgeon divides the lesser omentum close to the gastric wall, extending the division above the first branch of the crow's foot and beyond the pylorus into the first portion of the duodenum. The duodenum is transected with an endo-GIA echelon-sixty stapler using blue staples, and the transected duodenum and pylorus are retracted superiorly. Adhesions on the posterior aspect of the stomach are cleared, and the stomach is transected using an endo-GIA echelon stapling device with green staples and peristrips, requiring three applications. The nasogastric tube is retracted to the gastroesophageal junction, and the omentum is displaced. The transverse colon is lifted, and the ligament of Treitz is identified. The proximal jejunum is brought anticolic to the remaining gastric pouch.

Two stay sutures are placed in a serosal muscular fashion between the jejunum and the posterior wall of the remaining gastric pouch, avoiding tension. The proximal jejunum is oriented towards the greater curvature and the distal part towards the lesser curvature. An opening is created with electrocautery in the posterior gastric wall and the anterior jejunal antimesenteric wall. The endo-GIA echelon-sixty with a blue cartridge is introduced into the jejunum and stomach, advanced, closed, and fired, with the staple line inspected for bleeding, showing none.

The surgeon closes the opening in the stomach and jejunum using continuous threo-PDS sutures, incorporating all layers. A second serosomuscular interrupted layer with threoethabond is added to reinforce the closure. The afferent loop of the gastrojejunostomy is fixed to the greater curvature of the stomach with an additional suture to prevent kinking, and a similar fixture is applied to the descending limb. An air leak test is performed to ensure the airtightness of the anastomosis.
